skill.name: multi-agent-patterns
skill.description: Guides the design of multi-agent AI systems using supervisor, swarm, and hierarchical coordination patterns.
skill.summary: Design multi-agent systems with clear coordination, context isolation, handoffs, and failure controls.
skill.author: ChakshuGautam

security_audit.summary: AI review dismisses the critical heuristic, weak-crypto, certificate/key, network, and external-command detections as false positives caused by Markdown fences, diagrams, framework examples, URLs, and dictionary key access. The only confirmed concern is documentation-level file-system coordination sample code that constructs paths from dynamic values; it is not executed by the skill but should be treated as implementation guidance that needs path validation.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Examples Use Dynamic File Paths
medium_findings.description: The file-system coordination and checkpoint examples read and write JSON files using workspace, key, resource, or workflow identifiers in constructed paths. This is not executed by the skill, but implementations copied from the examples should validate and constrain paths to prevent path traversal or unintended overwrite.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations and dynamic path construction are present, but they appear only in Markdown reference examples. This supports a moderate documentation-level risk, not a runtime exploit.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external-command locations are fenced code blocks, diagrams, or example snippets. No shell execution, subprocess call, Ruby backtick command, or user-controlled command invocation was found in the scanned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown formatting or illustrative Python code. There is no command execution API or shell invocation in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Reference Links and Example Tool Names
low_findings.description: The network detections are external documentation links and example words such as web search or browse in an architecture diagram. The skill does not make network requests or exfiltrate data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are human-readable references to framework documentation and a paper. No HTTP client call or outbound request code was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Certificate Findings Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic primitives, hashing, encryption, certificates, or private keys. The certificate/key alert maps to dictionary key selection logic, not secret material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found architecture prose and Python dictionary access at the cited locations. No evidence of cryptographic algorithm use or credential file access was found.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristics Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The analyzer combined false-positive shell, network, credential, and file-system signals into critical heuristics. Manual review found no malicious intent, no prompt injection, no data exfiltration, and no executable network or shell behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying high-risk signals do not hold after context review. The skill is educational guidance plus a local coordination helper, with no confirmed malicious chain.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path Construction in File-System Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Reference examples build paths with string interpolation before reading or writing files. Any production implementation should normalize paths, restrict writes to a trusted workspace, and validate identifiers before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is risky when implemented with untrusted identifiers, but here it is only a documentation sample. Review is warranted for users adapting the pattern.

content.value_statement: Complex AI workflows often fail when one agent must manage every context, tool, and decision. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design coordinated agent systems with clear roles, handoffs, and recovery controls.
actual_capabilities: Explains supervisor, swarm, and hierarchical multi-agent architecture patterns.
Provides guidance for context isolation and task decomposition across agents.
Describes handoff protocols, direct response forwarding, and coordination tradeoffs.
Covers consensus methods such as weighted voting and structured debate.
Includes a Python coordination helper for messages, supervisors, handoffs, voting, and failure handling.
Links framework-specific reference examples for LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI-style designs.
limitations: It is guidance and sample code, not a complete production agent platform.
Framework examples use placeholder functions that require real implementations.
File-system coordination examples need path validation before production use.
It does not choose models, deploy services, or manage cloud infrastructure automatically.

use_cases.title: Plan a Research Agent Team
use_cases.description: Break a complex research task into researcher, analyzer, verifier, and writer agents with clear aggregation rules.

use_cases.title: Compare Agent Orchestration Patterns
use_cases.description: Evaluate when to use a supervisor, swarm, or hierarchy based on control, latency, and failure behavior.

use_cases.title: Add Handoffs to an Existing Assistant
use_cases.description: Design explicit transfer rules so specialized agents can pass context without losing important state.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a multi-agent architecture for this workflow. Compare supervisor, swarm, and hierarchical patterns, then recommend one with reasons: [describe workflow].

prompt_templates.title: Define Agent Roles
prompt_templates.prompt: Break this task into specialized agents. For each agent, define its objective, context inputs, tools, output format, and handoff rules: [task].

prompt_templates.title: Review Coordination Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this multi-agent design for bottlenecks, divergence, error propagation, consensus failures, and unnecessary token cost: [paste design].

prompt_templates.title: Design Failure Recovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production recovery plan for this agent workflow, including timeouts, retries, circuit breakers, checkpoints, validation gates, and escalation paths: [workflow].

output_examples.input: Design an agent system for market research and report writing.
output_examples.output: A supervisor-based plan with separate research, analysis, fact-checking, and writing agents. The output includes handoff rules, validation checks, and a final synthesis step.

output_examples.input: Review my proposed swarm architecture for customer support triage.
output_examples.output: A risk review that flags unclear ownership, missing convergence rules, and weak escalation handling. It recommends explicit state transfer and service-level limits.

output_examples.input: Help me add consensus to a group of review agents.
output_examples.output: A weighted voting design with confidence scoring, domain expertise weights, disagreement handling, and a debate round for high-impact decisions.

best_practices: Use multiple agents only when context isolation or parallel work clearly helps.
Define explicit handoff contracts, output schemas, and validation gates before implementation.
Test bottlenecks, stalled loops, bad handoffs, and downstream error propagation.
anti_patterns: Creating many role-named agents when one well-scoped agent would be simpler.
Letting agents pass large unfiltered histories instead of focused task context.
Using majority voting without confidence weighting or independent critique.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when a task needs multiple AI agents, context partitioning, parallel work, or explicit handoff design.

faq.question: Does it implement a complete framework?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance, reference examples, and a small coordination helper.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it discuss?
faq.answer: It discusses LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI-style patterns at a conceptual and example level.

faq.question: Can it help reduce token cost?
faq.answer: It can help avoid wasteful coordination, but multi-agent systems often use more tokens than single-agent workflows.

faq.question: Is the included Python file safe to run?
faq.answer: The Python helper is local coordination code and does not perform network, shell, credential, or file operations.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review path handling, retry limits, handoff contracts, validation rules, and observability for every agent workflow.
